mission.title: kubernetes: Add statefulset.kubernetes.io/pod-ordinal label
mission.description:
**What this PR does / why we need it**:
This PR makes the ordinal number of each pod in a statefulset available with the label `statefulset.kubernetes.io/pod-ordinal`.

This enables direct access to each pod’s ordinal number as an environment variable via the downward API:

```yaml
env:
- name: POD_ORDINAL
  valueFrom:
    fieldRef:
      fieldPath: metadata.labels['statefulset.kubernetes.io/pod-ordinal']
```

**Which issue(s) this PR fixes**:
Fixes #30427 and fixes #40651 

**Special notes for your reviewer**:

**Release note**:

```release-note
StatefulSet controller will create a label, `statefulset.kubernetes.io/pod-ordinal`, for the ordinal number of each Pod in a StatefulSet. This enables access to the ordinal number of each Pod controlled by a StatefulSet via the downward API.
```

resolution.summary:
> @liggitt do you have an opinion on bypassing alpha for this label? I think I'd say we follow field rules here. We _could_ put the label behind a feature gate for one release, which also accomplishes this goal.

I assume you mean whether to add the label at all, rather than whether to include ".alpha." in the label key?

Because the fields are in metadata, there's no loss of data in skew or downgrade scenarios.

On upgrade, will the controller add the label to existing pods?
steps: Review the issue discussion for context
Apply the fix from the linked pull request
codeSnippets:
env:
- name: POD_ORDINAL
  valueFrom:
    fieldRef:
      fieldPath: metadata.labels['statefulset.kubernetes.io/pod-ordinal']

/assign @kow3ns
/kind feature
/assign @janetkuo
@dixudx I certainly agree that it is trivial to parse the ordinal number from the pod name. It is a usability concern, however, as outlined by many comments in the linked github issues. Directly accessing the ordinal number as an environment variable is less brittle (every user does not need to implement their own logic) and more flexible (no need to override the entrypoint or use pod lifecycle events in order to support "off the shelf" images as communicated in comments on linked issues).
/cc @kubernetes/sig-apps-feature-requests
@jlegrone: Reiterating the mentions to trigger a notification: 
@kubernetes/sig-apps-feature-requests


<details>

In response to [this](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/68719#issuecomment-422421415):

>/cc @kubernetes/sig-apps-feature-requests


Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>
/ok-to-test

This makes sense to me, having written my fair share of annoying entrypoint wrapper scripts to parse the Pod name via `$(hostname)` into an ordinal env var. The addition of the `pod-name` label helped a little, but you still need the ugly wrapper script to parse the Pod name if you want the ordinal, and I've seen many people get this wrong (e.g. their scripts get confused if the StatefulSet name itself also contains a match for `-(\d+)`).

For anyone wondering why we couldn't have just added `pod-ordinal` to begin with instead of `pod-name`, it's because `pod-name` addresses a different use case. The purpose of `pod-name` was to have a label selector `pod-name: blah-N` that's guaranteed to select only one Pod in a given namespace. By contrast, a selector built from (StatefulSet's label selector + `pod-ordinal: N`) is not guaranteed to only select one Pod because multiple StatefulSets' label selectors can overlap or even be identical.

I think it makes sense to have both labels. The ordinal of a StatefulSet Pod meets the criteria to be a label because it's part of the identity of the Pod, and the linked issues show that it's useful.

Does anyone have any remaining objections to this?
@smarterclayton you were involved in the previous discussions on the topic. Does the api look good to you?
/hold

while we resolve the alpha API question @kubernetes/api-reviewers 
Can you articulate the use case for selecting by an ordinal as justification for this not just being an annotation (which is a smaller API change)?
> Can you articulate the use case for selecting by an ordinal as justification for this not just being an annotation (which is a smaller API change)?

@smarterclayton I don't think it will ever be a common case, but if a user (or more likely a CRD controller) is orchestrating several statefulsets to work together in some way, for example to represent replicated shards in a database, then it may be desirable to select a cross-section of pods controlled by those statefulsets.

Given this topology:

| sfs `shard-a`             | sfs `shard-b`             | sfs `shard-c`             |
|---------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| master (pod `shard-a-0`)  | master (pod `shard-b-0`)  | master (pod `shard-c-0`)  |
| replica (pod `shard-a-1`) | replica (pod `shard-b-1`) | replica (pod `shard-c-1`) |
| replica (pod `shard-a-2`) | replica (pod `shard-b-2`) | replica (pod `shard-c-2`) |

Users could leverage a label to view the state of only `replica` pods:

`kubectl get pods -l tier=db,statefulset.kubernetes.io/pod-ordinal!=0`

Or define a service to send traffic only to `master` pods:
